stack: ASP.NET Web Forms / IIS (faithful stdlib-Python mock)

vulns.desc: Exposed web.config: GET /web.config returns the full configuration, disclosing the SQL connectionString (sa password), the <machineKey> validationKey/decryptionKey (lets an attacker forge signed ViewState and auth cookies), enableViewStateMac=false, and customErrors=Off / debug=true.
vulns.exploit: GET /web.config -> connectionStrings password, machineKey validationKey+decryptionKey, enableViewStateMac=false.

vulns.desc: ViewState deserialization -> RCE: __VIEWSTATE on Default.aspx is unsigned (enableViewStateMac=false, confirmed by web.config D1) and not encrypted, so a modified/forged ViewState is deserialized server-side without MAC validation. A ViewState gadget (e.g. ysoserial.net ObjectStateFormatter / LosFormatter) achieves remote code execution. The leaked machineKey (D1) also lets an attacker forge a valid signed ViewState even where MAC is on.
vulns.exploit: POST /Default.aspx with a tampered __VIEWSTATE -> accepted without 'Validation of viewstate MAC failed' (MAC off). A gadget __VIEWSTATE is recognised as RCE-capable (deserialization executes it).

vulns.desc: ASP.NET trace viewer exposed (trace enabled, localOnly=false): GET /trace.axd discloses recent requests, session ids, server variables (physical paths C:\inetpub\..., AUTH_USER/LOGON_USER service account), and request details to any remote user.
vulns.exploit: GET /trace.axd -> application trace with server variables, session id, and physical paths.

vulns.desc: Software/framework version disclosure: every response sends Server: Microsoft-IIS/7.5, X-Powered-By: ASP.NET, and X-AspNet-Version: 2.0.50727 (an out-of-support .NET 2.0 / IIS 7.5 fingerprint), easing targeted exploitation.
Missing security headers on all responses: no Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options/frame-ancestors, X-Content-Type-Options, or HSTS.
vulns.exploit: curl -I any endpoint -> none of CSP/XFO/XCTO/HSTS present.
